activity_data.text:
We are deeply saddened to confirm the death of one of our own, Spc. Sarah Beckstrom, a member of the West Virginia Army National Guard, who was shot in the line of duty near the Farragut Square Metro Station on November 26, 2025. Spc. Beckstrom was pronounced dead at MedStar Washington Hospital in the District of Columbia on November 27, 2025, as the result of wounds incurred during the shooting.

Spc. Beckstrom enlisted in the West Virginia National Guard on June 26, 2023, and served with distinction as a military police officer with the 863rd Military Police Company. She exemplified leadership, dedication, and professionalism.

Spc. Beckstrom, a 2023 graduate of Webster County High School, was a resident of Summersville, West Virginia. She volunteered to serve as part of Operation D.C. Safe and Beautiful helping to ensure the safety and security of our nation’s capital. Her loss is felt profoundly across our One Guard Family and throughout the Mountain State.

“On behalf of Governor Patrick Morrisey and the entire West Virginia National Guard, I extend my deepest condolences to Spc. Beckstrom’s family, friends, and fellow Guardsmen. We grieve alongside them and honor her memory by carrying forward her commitment to service, integrity, and excellence.” said Maj. Gen. Jim D. Seward, the Adjutant General of the West Virginia National Guard.

We ask that everyone respect the privacy of her family and her fellow Soldiers during this difficult time.

article.title: Millionaire investor linked to China poison case

activityOfUser.occupation: Software Engineer

activity_data.text:
A property investor living in a small NSW town stands accused of being at the centre of a mysterious and deadly poisoning that has enthralled millions in China for 30 years.

Sightings of the woman known as Shiyan “Jasmine” Sun, in Port Stephens, on the northern central NSW coast, have sparked a petition for Anthony Albanese to deport her to China to face questions over the case.

Ms Sun and husband Feiyu “Ringo” Xie have built up a multimillion-dollar property portfolio in the region, but according to campaigners seeking justice for her alleged victim, she is the prime suspect in the 1994 poisoning of her university roommate, Zhu Ling. This is the first time Jasmine's been pictured in 20 years.

Zhu died a month ago, turning the long-running tragedy into a potential homicide investigation.

activityOfUser.occupation: Software Development Engineer 2 (SDE) at Microsoft

activity_data.text:
"We want to hire top 0.1% engineers"

I'm hearing this a lot right now. 

But what does that actually mean any more?

Back in 2015 that was simple, it meant they had a Computer Science degree from a Top 10 global university, they had done internships at Meta / Palantir / Google / Slack / Dropbox etc  during their degree, they'd competed in Olympiads and that they worked at high bar tech company, FAANG / HFT / T1 Start ups etc. 

They could fizz buzz in their sleep, wrote clean performant code and could knock up a whiteboard design of Slack from first principles with minimal prompts.

I think it's different now.

When I’m speaking to founders they’re looking for things you can’t see on paper

🤝They want ownership
 🏃They want hustle
🧘They want low ego
😗👌They want product taste

They want to see entrepreneurial energy, they want to see engineers that vibe code side hustles on the weekend, that are prepared to throw themselves into something.  

The problem is, most companies don't know these abilities even when they're sitting in their inbox. The orthodoxy of hiring misses them all together.

This is the problem we are spending every moment obsessing over here Dex

Link in the comments to sign up...

document.title: Intel AMX Docs for SimSIMD Contributors

activityOfUser.occupation: Software Development Engineer 2 (SDE) at Microsoft

activity_data.text:
Most modern AI boils down to matrix multiplications. Yet, fewer than 100 people worldwide have the practical experience to implement them efficiently across multiple platforms.

Since 2018, I've been working with tiled Matrix Multiplication instructions, starting with NVIDIA Titan Volta GPUs. It's frustrating to see how little things have improved. With custom instructions from CPU vendors — Intel Corporation AMX, Apple AMX, Arm SME, and more — each comes with its own complexities, making performance optimization harder, not easier.

It seems like CPU vendors are competing to create the most obscure Instruction Set Architecture (ISA) rather than focusing on Tensor Operations Per Second (TOPS). Take Intel AMX, for example — its 25-page documentation lacks details, like CPU support, compiler support, or even instruction latency/throughput!

At times, performance-oriented programming feels like hacking and reverse engineering!

---

If you're up for the challenge, check out the AMX manual and consider contributing to SimSIMD (https://lnkd.in/d9M9T_cH).

🎤 I'm speaking at at 𝐊𝐮𝐛𝐞𝐂𝐨𝐧 + 𝐂𝐥𝐨𝐮𝐝𝐍𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞𝐂𝐨𝐧 𝐄𝐮𝐫𝐨𝐩𝐞 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟔 in Amsterdam!

𝐙𝐞𝐫𝐨-𝐃𝐨𝐰𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐦𝐞 𝐌𝐢𝐠𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐟𝐫𝐨𝐦 𝐢𝐧𝐠𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐬-𝐧𝐠𝐢𝐧𝐱 𝐭𝐨 𝐈𝐬𝐭𝐢𝐨 𝐢𝐧 𝐚 𝐌𝐮𝐥𝐭𝐢-𝐂𝐥𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐫 𝐊𝐮𝐛𝐞𝐫𝐧𝐞𝐭𝐞𝐬 𝐏𝐥𝐚𝐭𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐦 𝐚𝐭 𝐁𝐥𝐨𝐨𝐦𝐛𝐞𝐫𝐠

ingress-nginx is being retired. Best-effort maintenance ends at the end of this Month. After that, no releases, no bug fixes, and no security patches. Your deployments will keep running, but you'll be on your own. Any CVE discovered after retirement goes unpatched. If ingress-nginx is still handling your production traffic, that's a risk you can't afford to ignore.

In this talk, I'll share how we migrated from ingress-nginx to Istio across Bloomberg's large-scale multi-cluster Kubernetes platform built atop Karmada, without any downtime. I'll cover the incremental rollout strategy, the challenges of preserving routing semantics at scale, and the lessons learned along the way. If you're planning a similar migration, this one's for you.

📅 Tuesday, March 24 | 15:00 – 15:30 CET
📍 Hall 1–5 | Tram Zone | Cloud Native Theater
👉🏾 Register here: https://lnkd.in/ekKMiZ6j

See you in Amsterdam! 👋🏾

#KubeCon #CloudNativeCon #Kubernetes #Istio #Nginx #Karmada #MultiCluster #CNCF

I am retiring from Microsoft. It's time. The team is looking to back fill my position and the position will be on the Microsoft Careers site shortly. (Don't ask. You don't want to know.) I have worked for Microsoft twice in my 45-year career for a total of 13+ years You always learn things at Microsoft scale, sometimes uncomfortable things, but it has always been a rewarding time and I have always been well treated (even in the bad old days of 25 years ago). 

I am continuing to teach the open source software engineering course at Carnegie Mellon University School of Computer Science, as well as working with Sayeed Choudhury on Sloan Foundation funded work. So I am still around causing trouble, just no longer working for Microsoft.

activity_data.text:
OKE Virtual Hiring Event – Software Developers (Nashville)

We’re growing the OCI Kubernetes Engine (OKE) team in Nashville and we’re hosting a virtual hiring event for experienced engineers.

We have several openings for:
- Senior Member of Technical Staff (SMTS)
- Principal Member of Technical Staff (PMTS)

This event will be hosted by me and my peer hiring managers across the organization. We all build the data plane of OKE—where the real scale, performance, and reliability challenges live.

About OKE
OCI Kubernetes Engine (OKE) is the managed Kubernetes service on Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI). We help customers run production Kubernetes clusters with enterprise-grade security, availability, and operational simplicity.

What we’ll cover
- A look at the OKE data plane: what we build and why it matters 
- The types of problems we’re solving (scale, resiliency, networking, upgrades, multi-tenancy, observability) 
- Roles we’re hiring for in Nashville and what “great” looks like at SMTS/PMTS levels 
- Q&A with hiring managers + next steps in the interview process

Who should join
You’re a strong fit if you have experience with some of:
- Distributed systems and cloud services
- Kubernetes and/or container platforms
- Networking, service reliability, performance engineering
- Languages such as Go/Java (plus strong systems fundamentals)

Interested?
Register: https://lnkd.in/gCAqEyDH
Date/Time: February 5, 2026 at 5:00 PM - 6:30 PM Central Time
Location: Virtual | Roles located in Nashville

Know someone great? Please share.

article.title: GitHub - MikeZappa87/dra-example

activityOfUser.occupation: Principal Software Engineer Lead - Istio TOC

activity_data.text:
Confused by Kubernetes Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA)? I built a simple demo to clarify its mechanics.

Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) is now generally available in Kubernetes 1.35. However, with components like ResourceSlices, ResourceClaims, DeviceClasses, and CDI specs, it can be overwhelming. To simplify this, I created a basic example. A DRA driver that treats a file as a "device." 

This example uses no GPUs or FPGAs or network resources, just a file located at /etc/dra/file1. Why a file? It removes hardware complexity, allowing you to focus on understanding the DRA mechanics:

- How drivers publish devices via ResourceSlices
- How the scheduler matches pods to nodes
- How kubelet prepares devices through gRPC calls
- How CDI injects the device into containers

Once you grasp these patterns with a simple file, the mental model can easily transfer to real hardware like GPUs or Network devices.

The README includes architecture diagrams that explain the publish-subscribe pattern that drives DRA. 

You can check it out here: https://lnkd.in/gAjNpeP4

I hope this helps others better understand DRA. 

Note: I will be updating the demo, to include features around the latest runc, cdi and containerd to better support network devices on a different branch. Thanks Antonio Ojea for pointing this out!

#kubernetes #k8s

resharedPost.text:
Big week for **LMCache** ahead at #KubeCon in Atlanta! 🎉

We’re thrilled to share that Tensormesh and LMCache Lab will be joining as co-speaker for three talks throughout the week — diving deep into how KV caching is revolutionizing LLM inference performance and cost efficiency.

💡 Monday: I’ll join Martin Hickey from IBM to unpack how **LMCache** an drastically cut inference costs through caching.

⚙️ Tuesday: Together with collaborators from NVIDIA, IBM Research, Google, nd Red Hat, we’ll go hands-on in a deep-dive tutorial benchmarking vLLM inference speed — and show why KV caching (like LMCache) is the key to unlocking scalable, affordable LLM serving.

⚡ Wednesday: Yuhan Liu, our founding member and core contributor to vLLM production stack and LMCache, will join her collaborator, Suraj Deshmukh at Microsoft to explain how kv caching makes LLMs blazingly fast and cost-effective in production.

Come say hi 👋 at our booth **2142** — we’d love to show you how Tensormesh can help scale your inference demand while slashing costs.

Let’s make LLMs faster, cheaper, and everywhere. 💥

#KubeCon #AI #LLM #Inference #vLLM #LMCache #Tensormesh #NVIDIA #IBM #Microsoft #RedHat

article.title: Release v0.3.1 Release · llm-d/llm-d

activityOfUser.occupation: Principal Software Engineer Lead - Istio TOC

activity_data.text:
🚀 llm-d v0.3.1 is Here: Now with ARM Support and Microsoft Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) Integration!

This latest patch builds on the momentum from our v0.3.0 release, delivering several key enhancements focused on expanding hardware compatibility, broadening cloud-native support, and improving the overall developer experience.

We're particularly excited about a few key updates:
💻 ARM Support: llm-d now supports ARM architectures! This is a significant step forward, enabling users to run and manage large language models on a wider, more diverse range of hardware.

☁️ AKS Cloud Provider Support: We've officially added Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) as a "well-lit" infrastructure provider. This makes it easier than ever for teams on Azure to deploy, manage, and scale their LLM workloads with llm-d.

🔧 Streamlined Image Builds: We've refactored our image build process into scripts and unified the GKE image into our core Cuda image. This simplifies maintenance, improves consistency, and makes the build process more transparent for our community.

This release also includes important bug fixes and stability improvements, thanks to the hard work of our dedicated team and community.

A special thank you to all our contributors, and a warm welcome to the many new faces who joined us for this release cycle. Your contributions are what make open-source projects like llm-d thrive.

For the full list of changes, bug fixes, and new contributors, check out the complete release notes on GitHub.

Full Changelog: https://lnkd.in/eAeytEAK

Join the community!  https://lnkd.in/eYUA98Ki

Absolute privilege sitting down with Brendan Burns, co-founder of Kubernetes and Corporate VP at Microsoft, at Ray Summit 2025!

From the rise of cloud-native with Kubernetes to today’s shift toward AI-native, Brendan has been at the forefront of how we build and scale modern computing systems

We talked all things open source, his journey, the evolution of Kubernetes, the emerging AI compute stack, and about a VERY exciting announcement:

👉 𝗔𝗻𝘆𝘀𝗰𝗮𝗹𝗲 𝗶𝘀 𝗻𝗼𝘄 𝗮𝘃𝗮𝗶𝗹𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗮𝘀 𝗮 𝗳𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁-𝗽𝗮𝗿𝘁𝘆 𝗼𝗳𝗳𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗼𝗻 𝗔𝘇𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗞𝘂𝗯𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗲𝘁𝗲𝘀 𝗦𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗰𝗲 (𝗔𝗞𝗦) 🎉

What this means?
This new service (currently in private preview) lets developers:
 • Access Anyscale natively within Azure
 • Launch Ray-powered clusters directly from the Azure Portal

 • Integrate with Azure Entra ID and IAM for unified governance
 • Get consolidated billing through Azure
 • Benefit from Anyscale Runtime optimizations for faster, more cost-efficient AI workloads

Learn more about the announcement here: https://lnkd.in/e5yUaw3G

…and stay tuned for the video podcast where Brendan shares his insights on the Anyscale channels!

article.title: Research: eBPF Can Actually Slow Your Applications

activityOfUser.occupation: Principal Software Engineer Lead - Istio TOC

activity_data.text:
ACM has a new paper on #eBPF, which reiterates what the #Istio project has said for years about this technology: we'll adopt it when it's actually faster than user space.  

While certain specific applications saw substantial improvements, partial offload scenarios (such as mTLS and HTTP processing in a service mesh) saw no effect on latency and reduced overall throughput. eBPF is a powerful tool in specific scenarios, but can actually be harmful to your service mesh.

article.title: Observability in Foundry Control Plane: Empowering Developers to Evaluate and Optimize AI Agents | Microsoft Community Hub

activityOfUser.occupation: Senior Software Engineer | GenAI Evaluation Platform @ Microsoft

activity_data.text:
🚀 Big news as we closed out Microsoft Ignite last week!

We’re thrilled to announce Observability in Foundry Control Plane, a game-changer for developers building and managing AI agents at scale.

Why does this matter? AI agents are powerful—but unpredictable. Ensuring trust, safety, and performance requires deep visibility across their lifecycle. 

That’s where Observability in the Microsoft Foundry comes in with the following capabilities:

✅ Integrated & Continuous Evaluations – Out-of-the-box and custom evaluators, synthetic datasets, and cluster analysis to accelerate reliable agent development.
✅ Unified Monitoring Dashboards – Track cost, performance, and safety with actionable insights.
✅ End-to-End Tracing – OpenTelemetry-based tracing for debugging every agent run.
✅ Fleet-Wide Oversight – Observe agents across Foundry and third-party frameworks in one view.
✅ AI Red Teaming Agent – Automate safety testing to harden generative AI systems.

CarMax joined us on stage to share how these capabilities help them monitor and optimize their AI agent, Skye—boosting confidence and accelerating innovation.

With these tools, organizations can evaluate, monitor, and optimize every agent responsibly and efficiently—from prototype to production.

👉 Dive deeper into the details: Read the full blog post: https://lnkd.in/g3QevZsi

#MicrosoftIgnite #AzureAI #MicrosoftFoundry #Observability #AIInnovation

This moment has been months in the making, and it only happened because of the incredible people I’ve been lucky enough to build with.

Change doesn’t happen alone - it takes a group of people willing to show up, put in the work, and believe in something bigger than themselves. Every late night, every brainstorming session, every line of code, every design sketch - it all came from this volunteer team. And we wouldn’t be here without them.

In just 10 months, together we’ve:
-Built VoteMate with 50+ volunteers and Responsible AI leaders from Microsoft and elsewhere, supported by Microsoft for Startups.
-Collaborated with Carnegie Mellon University - Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy on a Summer R&D program and Fall Capstone.
-Established partnerships with trusted civic nonprofits like Democracy Works and Vote Early Day.
-Secured Patent Pending status on our personalized ballot-building AI system.
-Brought a Preview product to life on minimal funding and zero debt - driven entirely by the heart and hustle of volunteers.

This Preview release is an early step - focused first on New Jersey and Virginia for upcoming elections - but it’s proof of what’s possible when people across backgrounds, professions, and beliefs come together around a shared purpose.

How you can support:
- Explore the platform at votemate.io
- Share it with friends, family, your network
- Send us your feedback through the app - every bit helps us grow!

I want to take a moment to celebrate and thank every single person who got us here. Words are not enough to express my gratitude!

Core Team
Alex Saxerud, David Rosenthal, Jayanta (Jay) Sen, Joel Partridge, Liz (Elizabeth) Walsh, Sierra Dowd, Valerie Robert, Amanda Molina

Engineering
Aaron Sweeney Catherine Bell Christopher Sims Corey Donahue eric nichols Janhavi Mahajan juhyun lee Paul Flanagan Richard Pressler Sunil Gudivada

GTM/Marketing
Clara Cox, MPA, Lane Hummel, Emily Kitching, Mitali Modi, Jessica Blair, Johanna Petruski

Founding Partners
Lisa Sullivan, Dan Sullivan , Kya Sainsbury-Carter, Brian O'Kelley, Jonathan Mars Jeff Lowinger

Board Members
Dr. Jennifer King, Dave Leichtman, Udi Milo, Ryan Germany, Shelby Summerfield

This is just the beginning - but this team shows what’s possible when we come together to change the world for the better.

🚀 Here we go!

activityOfUser.occupation: Software Engineer 2 at Microsoft

activity_data.text:
📢 📢 Do you want to learn how to use Azure Firewall's 🛡 Auto-learn SNAT routes feature? This feature can help you optimize your network configuration in Azure by automatically learning the out-of-network address spaces from the Azure Route Server and applying these as non SNAT routes in the Azure Firewall Policy. In this blog, we will show you how to enable this feature in the Azure Portal, and how it works with your network virtual appliance and the Azure Route Server. You will also see the benefits of using this feature, and how it can save you time and effort in defining the SNAT private ranges. Auto-learn SNAT routes is a public preview feature that can enhance your network experience in Azure, so don't miss this opportunity and give it a try today! 📢 📢 

⭐ Special shout out to Daniel Mauser and his amazing NVA repos and walk throughs. If you'd like to use the same appliance as I did to build out my NVA in Azure as part of a POC, you can find his GitHub repos in the reference section of the blog. ⭐

activity_data.text:
I saw many engineers blaming the outage on Microsoft 🤦‍♂️ SWEs blaming without knowing the root cause is concerning.

It is not Microsoft, it is Crowdstrike who released an update for Windows that had a bug. The patch runs in Kernel mode to monitor system activity at a low level.

Because it was running in Kernel mode, the buggy code was trying to access an invalid memory location that triggered a panic and showed a Blue Screen of Death.

The name of the driver file that had the buggy update is "C-00000291.sys", deleting it fixes the issue, and unfortunately, this needs to be done manually.
 
Microsoft has nothing to do with it.

activity_data.text:
Koo an indigenous Bharatiya company & Twitter alternative is scheduled to shut down. 

But this isn't Bharat's first loss. 

Hike Messenger was launched in 2012 to rival WhatsApp. 

It was way ahead of its time and had better features than WhatsApp. 

-) Offline Messaging: First of its kind, WhatsApp doesn't have this even in 2024.
-) Video Calling: Hike launched it in September 2016, whereas WhatsApp launched in November 2016.
-) Voice Calling: Hike- January 2015, WhatsApp- March 2015.
-) Hike Direct: Launched in October 2015, this feature allowed users to share files without an internet connection using Wi-Fi Direct. WhatsApp still does not have an equivalent feature.
-) Hidden Mode: Hike has had this feature since 2014, WhatsApp introduced it 10 years later and still doesn't provide any encryption.
-) Stickers: Hike was one of the first messaging apps to introduce various stickers, including regional ones.
-) Timeline and Stories: Hike had its version of Stories and Timeline updates before WhatsApp introduced the Status feature in February 2017.

Hike died because Indians have an inherent bias towards foreign products. 

We suffer from colonial hangover and a Made in USA product implicitly gets preferred over a Made in India product. 

According to me, Hike Messenger's death is one of our greatest losses in the product space. 

We had a mammoth opportunity to tackle Meta and we lost it. 

Agree?

#hike #whatsapp #koo

resharedPost.text:
World's first Level-5 autonomous driving capability demo, where our autonomous vehicle learned to negotiate complex-traffic dynamics in the Toll-Plaza and learned to cross highly unstructured toll-gates. 

This is second only to our Nov-15 2017 and October 22 2023 demos, in terms of Level-5 capabilities, where we enabled #autonomousdriving for negotiating tight-stochastic-dynamic-adversarial environments via multi-RL agents, and bidirectional traffic negotiation on single lane roads, respectively. 

In pursuit of our Level-5 we started asking very tough questions: Can autonomous vehicles learn to cross toll-gates, that may be as unstructured as the ones in India, while also negotiating complex traffic-dynamics in toll-gate areas?

In this demo we showcase for toll-gate crossing and complex traffic negotiation at such intersections, while dealing with the highly stochastic behaviours of large trucks. This is the world's first and the only demo of #autonomousvehicles learning to perform maneuvers of such a complexity.

Our vehicle can be seen entering the toll-gate region through a highway, and then negotiating bidirectional traffic dynamics in an open area with literally no driving rules to adhere to in such regions. Truck drivers often park their trucks randomly at night in Toll-Plaza areas, which can be seen in the video, with numerous trucks parked there. Furthermore, our vehicle had to decide which toll-gate-passage to commit to, while negotiating the criss-cross navigation pattern of the trucks that over-took us.

To understand the level of complexity, a tractor tyre was placed behind a very-large 18-wheeler truck, the detection of which is very tough at night, and our vehicle successfully negotiated it, while a truck was overtaking us from the right.

Finally, our vehicle slowed and paused while approaching a speed-breaker and aligned itself with the toll-gate it committed to on-the-fly, and then crossed the toll-gate while adhering to the driving rule and speed limit at the toll-gate. Furthermore, the gate immediately left to us was barricaded with a broken traffic-police barricade, and our system detected it and adapted accordingly. 

We had developed a very-specific sophisticated motion planning and decision making algorithmic framework to execute this behaviour, and this framework is being further scaled with unsupervised deep learning, and in the month of February we will show an end-to-end negotiation of the day-time traffic.

#reinforcementlearning #deeplearning #machinelearning

activity_data.text:
With two consecutive news in the EV and autonomous vehicle industry first
1]BYD a big player from china overtook TESLA for its higher sales of electric vehicles  for year 2023.
2] Swaayatt Robots a Indian startup from Bhopal has successfully demonstrated world's first Level 5 autonomous driving capability.
They have automated  "Mahindra Bolero" by enabling  ADAS technology in it and demonstrated LEVEL 5 ADAS feature of crossing a Toll-Plaza successfully.
You can check the demo here:
https://lnkd.in/dQ5CunYM

So Lets Decode the Levels of ADAS:
What is ADAS?
Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) are technologies designed to enhance vehicle safety by assisting the driver in various tasks. These systems use sensors, cameras, radar, and other technologies to monitor the vehicle's surroundings and provide warnings or take corrective actions when necessary. ADAS can be categorized into different levels based on the level of automation and the extent to which the system can control the vehicle. 

Level 0 - No Automation
At this level, there is no automation, and the driver is fully responsible for controlling the vehicle.
Level 1 - Driver Assistance
Level 1 systems provide a single automated system, such as adaptive cruise control (ACC) or lane-keeping assistance.
The system can either control the vehicle's speed or steering, but not both simultaneously.The driver is required to remain engaged and monitor the vehicle's surroundings.
Level 2 - Partial Automation
Level 2 systems offer a combination of automated features, such as adaptive cruise control and lane-keeping assistance, which can work simultaneously.
The driver is still required to monitor the environment and be ready to take control at any time.
(Tesla's Autopilot is an example of a Level 2 system)
Level 3 - Conditional Automation:
At this level, the vehicle can perform certain driving tasks autonomously under specific conditions.
The driver can disengage from actively controlling the vehicle, but must be ready to take over when the system requests.
(Audi's Traffic Jam Assist is an example of a Level 3 system)
Level 4 - High Automation
Level 4 systems are designed to perform specific driving tasks autonomously without driver intervention in certain scenarios.
The vehicle can operate autonomously within defined conditions, such as highway driving or urban environments.
However, the driver may still need to take control in certain situations.
Level 5 - Full Automation
Level 5 represents full automation, where the vehicle is capable of performing all driving tasks autonomously without any human intervention.
There is no need for a steering wheel or pedals, as the vehicle is fully self-driving.
Level 5 autonomy envisions complete driverless transportation.

Congratulations Swaayatt Robots (स्वायत्त रोबोट्स) for this great milestone.

Dear Recruiters,

Warm greetings from PUCSD!!

We, Placement Cell, Pune University Computer Science Department, Pune are glad to announce the inception of outreach for placement session 2023-24.
The Placement Cell is gearing up for a great recruitment cycle later after this month.

Department of Computer Science, Pune University is identified as one of the earliest Computer Science Department in India. In year 2023 PUCSD complete it's 43 years of excellence in the field of Computer Science shaping the minds which can change the world, who are ever ready to face the complexities of Dynamic corporate environment.
Our Alumni are working at Major organizations such as Amazon, Google, Microsoft, Nvidia, Barclays, Citi, Accolite, Commvault, IBM etc.

We strongly believe that the placement process is not just a yearly affair, but is an ongoing alliance between the institute and the organizations. We would like to thank the industry for its kind patronage and successful association with us year after year and look forward to similar "tie-ups" in future. We have persistently met with the ever-rising standards of the industry with suitable changes in the curriculum and culture. Our regular recruiters include Nomura, Commvault, Nvidia, Accolite , SAP and Agrostar.
Department is ready with 100 + brilliant minds pursuing MCA and MSc CS for the placement drives.

MCA and Msc students graduating in 2024 will be available for final placements (full time job offers).

MCA students will be available for 4 to 6 months compulsory internship in their final semester and they can be converted to full time employees if company is offering PPO to them.

activity_data.text:
Excited to bring you a new blog: "Microsoft PostgreSQL OSS engine team: reflecting on 2024" (https://lnkd.in/gPthCzm9)

In this first part of a two part blog, you will learn about who the Microsoft PostgreSQL OSS Engine team is, their code contributions to upstream PostgreSQL, technical details behind those changes & their journey during 2024. 

In the second part which is coming soon, you will get a sneak preview of upcoming work in 2025 (and PG18 cycle) and more.

Thank you to the team: Andres Freund, Amit Langote, Ashutosh Bapat,  Daniel Gustafsson, David Rowley, Melanie Plageman, Mustafa Melih Mutlu, Nazır Bilal Yavuz, Rahila Syed, Thomas Munro & Tomas Vondra for all the  hard work and dedication you bring to PostgreSQL. Thank you to the amazing PostgreSQL development community for the great work, which makes PostgreSQL the best and the most loved database in the world. 

Thank you Claire Giordano, Teresa Giacomini for helping me polish the blog contents and Guy Bowerman for the final push to publish.

See you all soon with part 2: "Microsoft Postgres OSS engine team: previewing 2025" !

article.title: DOCUMENTAR RECORDER. Justiție capturată

activityOfUser.occupation: Software Engineer

activity_data.text:
Acest documentar de două ore, care comprimă o muncă de investigație de peste un an și jumătate, încearcă să explice mecanismele complicate prin care sistemul judiciar, un pilon cheie al oricărui stat democratic, a ajuns să fie capturat de un grup de interese format din magistrați și politicieni.

Într-o formă simplificată, pactul dintre cele două tabere arată așa: politicul a oferit legi care au creat o organizare piramidală a sistemului de justiție, punând toată puterea în mâinile unui grup restrâns de magistrați, iar această mână de magistrați a oferit la schimb o justiție care nu-i mai deranjează pe cei puternici.

Mărturiile pe care le-am adunat din interiorul sistemului de justiție și pe care le prezentăm în acest material sunt de o gravitate fără precedent. Ele vorbesc despre dosare de mare corupție în care judecătorilor li se sugerează să ia decizii favorabile inculpaților, despre încălcarea flagrantă a principiului repartizării aleatorii a dosarelor prin schimbarea discreționară a completurilor de judecată, despre cum magistrații care nu se supun sunt marginalizați, hăituiți prin anchete ale Inspecției Judiciare și uneori chiar excluși din magistratură.

Consecințele acestor realități dramatice ne afectează viețile tuturor, pentru că o societate democratică nu poate supraviețui fără o justiție independentă: https://lnkd.in/dvRbRRiQ

user_info.occupation: Software Engineer

resharedPost.text:
Hype Cycle for AI in 2023

Two areas that might be relatively less well-known, but are also quite interesting to check:

◼ Neurosymbolic AI - combines the strengths of symbolic AI and neural networks. Symbolic AI is based on the idea of representing knowledge in a formal way, using symbols and rules based on the use of logic and mathematics.

Neurosymbolic AI uses neural networks to learn the weights of symbolic rules, which allows it to make inferences and draw conclusions in a more precise and accurate way than other approaches, such as machine learning.

Google's LaMDA and DeepMind's AlphaFold are two of the sucessful use cases. LaMDA uses it to understand and generate natural language; AlphaFold uses it to predict the structure of proteins.

◼ First-principles AI / Physics-informed AI - based on the idea that the laws of physics are fundamental and can be used to understand and predict the behavior of the world.

FPAI models are typically built by combining mathematical models of the physical world with ML algorithms. The ML algorithms are used to learn the parameters of the mathematical models, which are then used to make predictions.

Climate modeling, medical diagnosis, energy production, etc, are some potential use cases of FPAI.

Image credit to Gartner

activity_data.text:
Is this the end of the 5-day week? 👀
 
 
The results are in and the report tells us that the UK’s four-day week pilot was a huge success! 🎉
 
Involving 61 companies and around 2,900 workers, this is the world’s largest four-day week trial to date. 
 
 Quick facts:

🤸‍♂️ 92% of the participating employers are sticking with the four-day week
 
 🤸‍♂️ Company revenue rose by 1.4% on average
 
 🤸‍♂️ 96% of workers prefer the four-day week
 
 🤸‍♂️ Employee stress levels dropped by 39%
 
 🤸‍♂️ Employee burnout rates fell by 71%
 
 🤸‍♂️ 54% of workers felt a reduction in negative emotions
 
 🤸‍♂️60% of employees with care responsibilities saw an improvement to their work-life balance, and 62% reported a positive impact to their social lives
 
 🤸‍♂️ 48% of employees feel more satisfied than when they first started
 
 🤸‍♂️ Sick days plummeted by 65%
 
 🤸‍♂️ Staff resignations dropped by 57%

By the end of the trial, 46% of workers said they’d only accept a five-day week job if they received a pay increase, and 15% said that no amount of money could entice them return to a five-day schedule.
 
Hard as some will most definitely try, you simply can’t argue with those stats! And for those who do wish to air their concerns, there are a couple of things worth noting first:
 
 👉 One size doesn’t fit all, which is why participating firms designed a policy tailored to factors such as their industry, organisational challenges, departmental structures, and company culture.
 
 This means a variety of four-day weeks were created, from the classic ‘Friday off’ model, to alternative structures like ‘staggered’, ‘decentralised’, ‘annualised’, and ‘conditional’.
 
 👉 To avoid any biased data, companies who took part come from a range of sectors and vary in size and headcount. 
 
So, what’s your verdict? Does today mark the beginning of the end of the five-day work week? Let us know your thoughts below, and be sure to click the link in the comments to read the full report! 👇

activity_data.text:
Don’t fall for false DeepSeek R1 news! Deepseek R1 made it to mainstream, but there are so many wrong takes and false information. ℹ️

> No, the training didn’t cost only ~$6M dollars; the compute for the base model (no RL) was equivalent to GPU hours worth $5.5M, excluding ablations, smaller runs, data generation, and any of DeepSeek R1 training.

> No, it's not a side project (maybe started as one). DeepSeek is backed and owned by High-Flyer, a Chinese hedge fund; in 2020, they managed assets of over 7 billion dollars, and their talent includes Olympic medalists in mathematics, physics, and informatics.

> No, they don’t just have a few GPUs. They have ~50k GPUs.

> The real Deepseek R1 is 671B MoE model that needs > 16x 80GB Memory (16x H100s).

> Yes, the Deepseek R1 671B is really good! And they do great open source and science work > 2 years already

> There are 6 “distilled” versions. They are fine-tuned Qwen and Llama on 800k samples, (NO RL). Thats not “r1”. The smallest one with 1.5B (Yes, you can run locally, but it’s not near R1).

> Yes, the hosted version on chat(.)deepseek(.)com might use your data to train new models (as per ToS of DS).

> Yes, Open Science and Source will benefit everyone in the long term! Hugging Face is working on a fully open reproduction pipeline

If you are curious how Deepseek R1 trained, I wrote a blog: https://lnkd.in/eKnT7bHC

This system redefines large-scale inference — 4,600+ NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra GPUs, 37 TB of unified memory, and NVIDIA Quantum-X800 InfiniBand networking that connects every GPU and CPU at massive scale with near-zero latency. Together, they unlock the performance OpenAI’s next-generation models demand.

activity_data.text:
The competition for Day 0 or even Day -1 slots in Indian campus recruitment is becoming intense, bordering on craziness.
Companies want to secure slots earlier than others and are offering incentives to colleges to do so. Colleges, in turn, are looking to capitalize on this situation by improving their recruitment records or securing sponsorships for college fests.
However, in this process, two important participants—the interviewers and the candidates—are being blatantly ignored. Recruitment is starting at midnight, often going into the early hours of the morning. Despite all recommendations and research, companies expect interviewers and candidates to make sharp decisions at 2 or 3 a.m., when they are sleep-deprived.
What would be lost if interviews started in the morning? Why have we come to accept this as normal?

'Joy of Reading' needs your help

“Reading opens an infinite world of ideas and sparks imagination in children all over the world. Gift a book and spread the joy of reading.”

This year we are enrolled in ‘Benevity Causes Portal’ which enables donations through your company’s giving portals. During this giving month, I request you to consider donating to 'Joy of Reading' and help us spread the Joy and fulfill our mission of “Enabling every child to immerse in the world of Books."

More about our work: At Joy of Reading (https://joyofreading.org/), we tailor our contributions to the specific needs of government schools. By supplying books that cater to the unique demographics of each school, we ensure that every child has access to literature that resonates with them. Beyond just providing books, we share our expertise on library setup, maintenance, and management. We also train librarians on their pivotal roles and effective methods to foster reading among children. Additionally, we offer insights on conducting children's reading level assessments, advise on executing a structured library hour, guide English teachers on utilizing the library as a teaching resource, and provide strategies to support struggling readers. Through these efforts, we empower government school teachers to manage libraries efficiently, cultivate reading habits in children, and assist those who face reading challenges.

activity_data.text:
A quick reminder for anyone who might need it:
(We all reach that point sometimes)

When you’re constantly drained, it shows in everything.
→ Your creativity fades.
→ Your productivity slows.
→ You become irritable, exhausted, and mentally worn out.

That’s not just pressure.
That’s the early stage of burnout.
Which is why protecting your energy is essential.

Here are a few ways to do it:
1) Create Clear Work Limits
↳ End your workday when it’s supposed to end.
↳ Don’t allow work to take over your evenings or weekends.

2) Protect Time Between Tasks
↳ Leave 10–15 minutes between meetings.
↳ Step away, breathe, and reset your focus.

3) Review Your Workload Each Week
↳ Be honest about how much you’re carrying.
↳ Identify pressure early before it becomes overwhelming.

4) Learn to Decline Respectfully
↳ Your time is valuable.
↳ If something isn’t critical, postpone it or pass it on.

5) Avoid Taking On Too Much
↳ You’re not expected to handle everything.
↳ Focus on doing your role well instead of doing everything.

6) Make Rest a Priority
↳ You’re human, not a machine.
↳ Quality sleep and mental downtime matter.

You can’t perform at your best when you’re running on fumes.
Pause.
Recover.

Realign.
Taking care of yourself isn’t a luxury.
It’s essential.
What are your thoughts?

♻️ If this resonated with you, share it with your network.

Follow Bagombeka Job for more posts like this 👀 
Credit: Rob Dance

activity_data.text:
I know, this is not great content for a Monday morning, 

especially, because this is about a layoff situation! :-)

But hear me out.

You may enjoy this scene as much as I did, and you may have a great takeaway!

George Clooney, a specialist in "employment termination"

Basically, a guy who tells the employees that they have been laid off, probably in a nice and positive tone (whether or not the receiver feels the same!)

We all start working to make a living, but eventually, we end up living our entire life that way, which is such an unfortunate thing!

We all can do a bit of planning and help ourselves to lead a better and bigger life, over time, gradually, with some sincere effort!

What do you think?

The scene is from the movie, Up In The Air!

#movie #takeaway #dreams #author #ganeshkuduva

activity_data.text:
While onsite at a client office today, I was inspired by their community service event held in the office.

🌟 Community = Growth.

One of the things I’ve grown to appreciate most in my career—especially in tech—is the incredible impact of community. Not just the communities we work in every day, but the ones we build, support, and invest in beyond our desks.

🤝 Whether it’s partnering with local universities (an effort I have found to be one of the most rewarding), mentoring folks that are early in their careers, volunteering at community events, or simply sharing with others what we’ve learned, outreach and connection isn’t a side project. It’s part of who we are as professionals, and we're all better for it.

Every time we step outside our work space bubble, we get fresh perspectives and get the opportunity to discover new ideas and help shape the next generation of builders and leaders. It’s extremely energizing, and what I've found is that the ripple effects of giving back—big or small—always seem to come full circle.

🚀 In a field that evolves as fast as tech does, staying connected to the broader community keeps us grounded, inspired, and reminded of why we do what we do.

💡 What's your favorite way to get involved in your local or broader industry's community?

activity_data.text:
People who seem fearless aren’t certain.

They just learned to move while afraid.

Most people don’t get stuck from lack of talent.
They get stuck protecting comfort instead of growth.

They wait to “feel ready.”
They avoid what might expose gaps.
Chase perfection instead of progress.

But here’s the truth:
Every confident person you admire once felt unqualified too.
They just showed up anyway.

Because courage doesn’t follow confidence -
It creates it.

Here’s how to build that courage 👇

1. Get comfortable being a beginner
Every master started clueless.
Stop pretending to know. Start asking to learn.

2. Growth starts where ego ends
The bravest words are “I don’t know.”
Every time you say them, a new door opens.

3. Speak up when you feel small
Imposter syndrome hates honesty.
Say it out loud - take its power back.

4. Collect lessons, not labels
You’re not “bad” at something - just early at it.
Every skill looks messy in chapter one.

5. Stay curious under pressure
Ego says “I should know this.”
Curiosity asks “What can I learn here?”

6. Surround yourself with stretchers
Be around people who make you rise, not retreat.
Comfortable rooms rarely create growth.

7. Shed what no longer fits
Each new level asks you to release- 
an old belief, habit,
or identity that no longer belongs.

You won’t always see the path.
Sometimes it only appears after you take the step.

That’s the paradox of growth:
Clarity follows motion -
Not the other way around.

So keep moving.
Progress is built, not found -
And courage is what makes it real.

♻ Repost to help someone move even while afraid.
➕ Follow Mike Leber for daily insights on leadership and growth.

Image credit: Rob Dance

activity_data.text:
There are two kinds of people in the world — those who understand binary, and those who don’t.

But binary isn’t just for developers. 

It’s the quiet code shaping everything around us — your phone, your car, your bank, even your morning playlist.

It’s the unseen rhythm behind how the modern world thinks, moves, and connects.
And yes… it’s what your robots speak fluently. 🤖

Cheers to the language that started it all.

PS: Be honest — which type are you? 😅

activity_data.text:
Welcome to the BSides South Jersey group, the scrappy underdog of the Northeast cybersecurity scene. While our neighboring cities in the North and the West host the big, bustling events, we're building something different here in the shadows of the Pine Barrens, home of the legendary Jersey Devil.

Just like the mythical beast that stalks our woods, we represent the raw, unpolished, and sometimes chaotic underbelly of infosec. We're a community for those who prefer hands-on hacking over corporate keynotes, for the researchers and students who operate on the fringes, and for the security enthusiasts who are ready to get their hands dirty.

This isn't about shiny presentations or polished sales pitches. It's about authentic conversations, deep-dives into vulnerabilities, and the kind of networking that happens organically. We're the BSides event that lives up to the original spirit of the movement: community-driven, accessible, and full of surprising discoveries.

Join us to find your tribe, share your insights, and connect with the real heroes of cybersecurity. After all, sometimes the most interesting things are found where the big-city lights don't reach.

article.title: Detecting and explaining structural changes in an evolving graph using a martingale

activityOfUser.occupation: Software Engineer @Xbox | TEDx Speaker | Tech Enthusiast | Maker | Adventurer | Mentor | Polymath

activity_data.text:
Excited to share that the pre-proof of our paper "Detecting and Explaining Structural Changes in an Evolving Graph using a Martingale" is now available in Pattern Recognition (Elsevier):
https://lnkd.in/ee8TKPdn

In this work we:
- Represent an evolving network as a sequence of graph snapshots and extract multiple centrality and spectral feature streams.
- Construct an exchangeability-test martingale for each feature using non-conformity scores and a principled betting function.
- Aggregate those into an additive martingale, whose threshold crossing via Ville's inequality provides a rigorous false-alarm-controlled change-point detector.
- Show that the exact Shapley value for each feature at detection time equals its martingale contribution—enabling precise, "closed-form" attribution without costly approximations like SHAP.

We validate on synthetic graph models (Erdős-Rényi, Barabási-Albert, Newman-Watts-Strogatz) and on the MIT Reality social-proximity dataset, demonstrating tight F₁/delay trade-offs and interpretable feature attributions for real-world network shifts.

activity_data.text:
Vibe coding in the enterprise? Proceed with caution—and clarity.

AI tools that turn plain English into working code are having a moment. “Vibe coding” promises speed, accessibility, and democratized development. But let’s not confuse code generation with software engineering.

Because code that works isn’t the same as code that lasts. Vibe-coded systems often:

1. Lack architecture
2. Ignore security and compliance
3. Hide logic in conversations, not documentation
4. Break under scale - with no blueprint to fix them

We have seen this story before. From Excel macros to shadow IT. Fast becomes fragile. Useful becomes unmaintainable. What starts as innovation becomes a liability.

But here’s the reframe: vibe coding does have a role. Just not as your production pipeline.

Use it for what it’s best at: rapid prototyping, interface sketching, and idea exploration. In a sandbox. With clear boundaries. And a handoff to real engineers when it’s time to scale.

Experiment freely. Deploy responsibly.

The key takeaway: Vibe coding is a sketchpad, not a scaffold. Use it to accelerate discovery - not to shortcut discipline.

Would love to learn from your experiences with vibe coding?


 #VibeCoding #AIinTech #ShadowIT #SoftwareEngineering #TechStrategy #LLM #EnterpriseAI #RapidPrototyping #InnovationVsSustainability #ResponsibleAI

article.title: GitHub Copilot coding agent for Jira is now in public preview · GitHub Changelog

activityOfUser.occupation: Senior Strategic Client Advisor – Agentic AI | DevX | Governance | Software

activity_data.text:
GitHub Copilot coding agent for Jira just hit public preview — and it quietly changes what a "sprint" looks like.
Assign a Jira issue to Copilot. It reads it. Implements it. Opens a draft PR. Reports back in Jira. Asynchronously. In the background. While your team focuses on the hard stuff.
The repetitive bug fixes, the documentation updates, the low-complexity backlog items that nobody wants to pick up — that's exactly where this shines.
And the best part? It still follows your existing PR review and approval rules. Your governance doesn't change. Your merge policies don't change. The human is still in the loop where it matters.
This isn't AI replacing engineers. This is AI absorbing the ticket queue so engineers can do engineering.
The teams that will win with this aren't the ones who adopt it fastest — they're the ones who redesign their backlog hygiene around it.

#GitHub #Copilot #Jira #AgenticAI #BacklogGrooming #EnterpriseArchitecture #SolutionArchitect #DevOps #SoftwareEngineering #FutureOfWork

activity_data.text:
🔐 Security is everyone’s responsibility. But how do we make it practical before the commit even happens?

We all say “shift security left”, yet many teams still rely on vague “check security” prompts. That is not structured, and it is definitely not intentional.

After attending today’s “Agentic AI for DevSecOps” session by Calin Lupas and Emmanuel Knafo at Microsoft #TechConnect, I have a solid goodie to share
👉 https://lnkd.in/ectYH8VC

This repo contains a set of custom GitHub Copilot agents, each tailored to specific security related tasks. A very practical way to introduce pre-commit security thinking into everyday developer workflows.

How to use them effectively:
- Good idea: copy these agents into the .github/agents folder of your repo
- Very good idea: place them in a shared .private-github repo at the org  level so they are available to everyone, both in IDEs and with the Coding agent
- Homework: convert these agents into agent skills so they can be reused across any agent you work with

This is how “security is everyone’s responsibility” becomes something developers can actually act on, consistently and early.

Morten Stange Bye, Else Tefre, Christian Tryti, Liselotte Bjerkvik, Haakon Hasli, Daniel Meppiel, Joel Norman, Jonas Helin, Nils-Ove Gamlem, Ane Mosnes Sandvik, Francesco Manni, Ömür Sert, Adil I.

#DevSecOps #GitHubCopilot #ApplicationSecurity #ShiftLeft #AINativeInfra

activityOfUser.occupation: Senior Strategic Client Advisor – Agentic AI | DevX | Governance | Software

activity_data.text:
You can now run GitHub Agentic Workflows in your SDLC with reproducible context dependencies using microsoft/apm-action GitHub Action.

GitHub Agentic Workflows lets AI agents run inside your CI/CD pipeline. Triggered on a PR. On a schedule. On a label change. At enterprise scale. The agent reads your code, makes decisions, and acts — over thousands of repos if you like.

But here's what happens at scale: the agent's behavior depends on prompts, instructions, and skills scattered across repos. Someone updates an instruction upstream. Your agent silently changes behavior. No diff. No review. No trace.

That's the gap. Your app's code is versioned and pinned. The agent's knowledge is not.

microsoft/apm-action closes that gap. It treats prompts and instructions as dependencies — versioned, locked, reproducible. The same agent with the same context, every run. Whether that's Tuesday at 2pm or Saturday at 3am.

We've enforced this discipline on code for decades. It was only a matter of time before agents needed it too.

Agents in CI/CD is a new Agentic SDLC frontier. Reproducibility and security is what will make it production-ready at scale.

microsoft/apm-action wraps Agent Package Manager (APM), both shipping as OSS, MIT-licensed community projects to help you manage agent configuration and context as dependency packages.

article.title: 60 million Copilot code reviews and counting

activityOfUser.occupation: Senior Strategic Client Advisor – Agentic AI | DevX | Governance | Software

activity_data.text:
One in five code reviews on GitHub now runs through Copilot.

60 million reviews since April. 12,000+ organizations have it on by default. What changed: Copilot code review moved to an agentic model that retrieves repo context, reads linked issues and PRs, and plans its review strategy before commenting. It remembers patterns across reviews. And in 29% of reviews, it says nothing at all -- no comments, no noise, just silence when there's nothing worth flagging.

One of the most common complaints about automated code review is noise. Comments nobody reads. Suggestions that miss the point of the change. GitHub's response: treat silence as a feature. If the agent can't find something consequential, it keeps quiet. When it does comment, it averages about 5.1 comments per review and attaches feedback to logical code ranges instead of single lines.

WEX is running it across every repository and shipping ~30% more code.
Their most active contributors are the heaviest Copilot users.

- 60 million Copilot code reviews: https://lnkd.in/gT7uiZSP
- WEX customer story: https://lnkd.in/g2w9UysH

activity_data.text:
Are you measuring GitHub Copilot usage… or real impact? 🤔

Many teams proudly track seats, active users, and suggestion acceptance in GitHub Copilot.

That’s adoption.

But leadership conversations are shifting toward something else:
👉 Is AI actually improving engineering performance?

This is where gh-devlake changes the game.

Built as a GitHub CLI extension, gh-devlake connects Copilot usage data with real delivery metrics across your engineering systems. More details and how to start: https://msft.it/6048Q5AMC

Instead of looking at AI stats in isolation, you can correlate:

- Copilot usage
- PR cycle time
- Deployment frequency
- Change failure rate
- Mean time to recovery

Now we’re talking about impact.

What I like about this approach is that it moves the conversation from opinion to evidence. You’re no longer asking whether AI “feels” productive. You’re analyzing how AI adoption aligns with actual delivery outcomes in your organization.

For engineering leaders, this is critical.
AI investments need defensible ROI narratives. gh-devlake gives you the foundation to build them using your own DevOps data.

We are entering the phase where AI in engineering is not about experimentation anymore. It is about measurable value.

Are you already correlating Copilot adoption with delivery metrics? Or still tracking usage alone?

Eldrick Wega, Morten Stange Bye, Haakon Hasli, Else Tefre, Christian Tryti, Majid Hajian, Pankaj Agrawal, Muhammad Daniyal (Dani), Chris Romp, Daniel Meppiel, Jonas Helin, Joel Norman, Adil I., Ömür Sert, Francesco Manni, Jaime De Mora

article.title: GitHub - microsoft/work-iq-mcp: MCP Server and CLI for accessing Work IQ

activityOfUser.occupation: Senior Strategic Client Advisor – Agentic AI | DevX | Governance | Software

activity_data.text:
GitHub Copilot CLI just hit GA! 🎉 

Not to over-hype, but I'm actually pretty excited about this. I've been using it since preview and it's become my go-to for anything that starts in a terminal. But the part that actually changed how I work isn't the coding.

I use Copilot CLI with WorkIQ and my work notes to close the loop between different silos. Meeting notes in one place, tasks in another, context scattered across emails and docs. Copilot CLI ties them together and helps me organize my thinking, not just my code. WorkIQ lets Copilot access meeting recaps and relevant documents to augment my notes.

Copilot CLI also has the concept of "infinite sessions." Sessions persist locally as structured state files, with compaction checkpoints and persistent artifacts. I've been running the same session for my notes for over a month with no degradation. Repository memory means Copilot learns your conventions and preferences across sessions, so each new session starts smarter than the last. It's not just "long conversations" but durable working memory.

Background delegation is the other one worth knowing about. Or tell Copilot CLI to kick of parallel tasks (like code reviews with Opus and Codex) and it'll run them in the background, updating you when they finish.

You can get really meta and open Copilot CLI in the terminal in VS Code. Or if you've been waiting for Copilot in vim or Emacs, here you go!

- Copilot CLI GA: https://lnkd.in/dds3dpVS
- WorkIQ MCP server: https://lnkd.in/dFXBAuzy

article.title: GitHub Agentic Workflows

activityOfUser.occupation: Senior Strategic Client Advisor – Agentic AI | DevX | Governance | Software

activity_data.text:
GitHub just introduced Agentic Workflows. Yes, you can now wire generative AI directly into your CI/CD.

Let me be very clear: There are parts of the SDLC where AI has no business making autonomous decisions. Generative AI is probabilistic. Your production pipeline should not be.

Use it for pattern recognition, cross checking, refactoring suggestions:
– Checking whether a PR actually implements the acceptance criteria
– Verifying that documentation and tests reflect the feature
– Opening cleanup PRs for code quality and structural improvements

Do not use it if the potential blast radius includes production, money, or regulatory exposure:
– Deployments
– Deciding whether a security, compliance, or production bug “really matters”
– Provisioning or modifying cloud infrastructure
– Anything that must be deterministic, auditable, and legally defensible

If you follow me, you know I am not anti AI. What I am is very serious about understanding where non deterministic systems belong.

CI/CD is the control plane of your business. Treat it like one.

activity_data.text:
𝗚𝗶𝘁𝗛𝘂𝗯 𝗖𝗼𝗽𝗶𝗹𝗼𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝗳𝗶𝗻𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝘂𝗻𝘁𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗜𝗗𝗘.
I’m currently building POLY𝖢𝖫𝖠𝖶 — a personal project where I'm using the 𝗚𝗶𝘁𝗛𝘂𝗯 𝗖𝗼𝗽𝗶𝗹𝗼𝘁 𝗦𝗗𝗞 to move AI assistance out of the code editor and into a fully autonomous agentic experience.
By leveraging the 𝗦𝗗𝗞 alongside 𝖠𝗓𝗎𝗋𝖾 𝖡𝗈𝗍 𝖲𝖾𝗋𝗏𝗂𝖼𝖾 and 𝖠𝗓𝗎𝗋𝖾 𝖢𝗈𝗆𝗆𝗎𝗇𝗂𝖼𝖺𝗍𝗂𝗈𝗇 𝖲𝖾𝗋𝗏𝗂𝖼𝖾𝗌, I’ve enabled Copilot to live in your messaging apps or even reach out via real-time phone calls. It’s no longer just a chatbot; it’s a self-extending agent with a native scheduling system for cron jobs.

𝗞𝗲𝘆 𝗖𝗮𝗽𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗲𝘀:
🔹 𝗦𝗲𝗹𝗳-𝗘𝘅𝘁𝗲𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴: The agent actually 𝗐𝗋𝗂𝗍𝖾𝗌 𝗂𝗍𝗌 𝗈𝗐𝗇 𝗌𝗄𝗂𝗅𝗅𝗌 at runtime. If it lacks a tool, it builds it. 
🔹 𝗣𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗸𝘀𝗽𝗮𝗰𝗲 & 𝗠𝗲𝗺𝗼𝗿𝘆: It maintains its own environment across sessions—allowing it to manage things like 𝗅𝗈𝖼𝖺𝗅 𝖲𝖰𝖫𝗂𝗍𝖾 𝖽𝖺𝗍𝖺𝖻𝖺𝗌𝖾𝗌 or complex file structures autonomously. 
🔹 𝗔𝘂𝘁𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗺𝗼𝘂𝘀 𝗦𝗰𝗵𝗲𝗱𝘂𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴: It manages its own tasks — using its 𝗈𝗐𝗇 𝖻𝗋𝗈𝗐𝗌𝖾𝗋 or maintaining repos — and reports back proactively via 𝖠𝗓𝗎𝗋𝖾 𝖡𝗈𝗍 𝖲𝖾𝗋𝗏𝗂𝖼𝖾. 
🔹 𝗔𝗻𝘆𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗔𝗰𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀: Use the TUI, Web UI, or have it 𝗉𝗁𝗈𝗇𝖾 𝗒𝗈𝗎 via 𝖠𝗓𝗎𝗋𝖾 𝖢𝗈𝗆𝗆𝗎𝗇𝗂𝖼𝖺𝗍𝗂𝗈𝗇 𝖲𝖾𝗋𝗏𝗂𝖼𝖾𝗌 when a critical deployment needs your attention.

⚠️ 𝖠𝗇 𝖤𝗑𝗉𝖾𝗋𝗂𝗆𝖾𝗇𝗍𝖺𝗅 𝖡𝗎𝗂𝗅𝖽: This is an independent, community-built tool. It is currently in an 𝖾𝖺𝗋𝗅𝗒 𝖾𝗑𝗉𝖾𝗋𝗂𝗆𝖾𝗇𝗍𝖺𝗅 stage. Polyclaw operates with a high degree of autonomy under 𝗬𝗢𝗨𝗥 identity. It carries your permissions and tokens; it’s built for developers who want to push the limits of agentic workflows while staying mindful of the risks.

If you’ve seen the recent traction around 𝖮𝗉𝖾𝗇𝖢𝗅𝖺𝗐, this is my take on a native evolution for those deep in the GitHub ecosystem.

I’m still deep in the development phase, so stay tuned — 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗼 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗲 𝘀𝗼𝗼𝗻.

activity_data.text:
🧭Cap sur l'Agentic DevOps avec GitHub Copilot !

Vendredi dernier, avec mes collègues de AXA en France , nous nous sommes plongés au cœur de l’écosystème GitHub Copilot  dans les locaux de Microsoft.

Un immense merci à Daniel Meppiel  et Thierry Madkaud  pour la qualité de leurs présentations. Leur expertise nous a permis de décrypter des concepts qui transforment déjà notre manière de concevoir le logiciel.

💡Les piliers technologiques abordés :
Le framework PROSE : Nous avons mis en pratique cette approche structurée qui permet d'industrialiser nos interactions avec l'IA. Finis les prompts aléatoires, place à une ingénierie rigoureuse pour des résultats plus fiables. https://lnkd.in/eBV3dgzY

Agentic Workflow & Continuous AI : L'IA ne se contente plus de suggérer du code, elle devient un partenaire autonome capable d'orchestrer des tâches complexes au sein du workflow de développement. https://githubnext.com/ 

APM (Agent Package Manager) & Copilot CLI : Focus sur la gestion propre des briques d'IA et découverte de la Ralph Wiggum loop pour une expérience en ligne de commande boostée. 

L'évolution vers l'Agentic DevOps : Une réflexion passionnante sur la mutation de nos pratiques. L'intégration d'agents intelligents redéfinit totalement le cycle de vie applicatif.

🛠️ Mise en pratique
Notre voyage s’est terminé par un hands-on concret : l'utilisation de ces outils pour documenter et tester un projet de code existant. La puissance de frappe combinée de Copilot et d'une méthodologie comme PROSE est impressionnante. (Merci Julien Strebler pour le devcontainer) 

Prêts à intégrer ces super-pouvoirs dans nos futurs projets chez AXA en France ! 🔥

François Descamps Sebastien DEGODEZ Olivier Sailly

Developer choice (in many ways) has always been at GitHub’s core. The agentic era is no different. The right IDE for your workflow, the right model for the challenge, the right agent for your task – all in the place where developers already build. ❤️ 

The best tools meet developers where they work. That’s always been the GitHub way, and it’s what we’ll keep building toward.  
 
Really proud to get this into the hands of developers quickly, and excited to hear what folks think.

activity_data.text:
Votre stratégie de documentation doit évoluer pour l'ère de l'IA.

Le constat : les assistants de code IA comme GitHub Copilot indexent vos repositories. Si votre documentation est dans Confluence ou Notion, elle est invisible pour les outils que vos développeurs utilisent au quotidien.

Les chiffres parlent d'eux-mêmes : 
→ Recherche DORA : les équipes avec une documentation de qualité ont 2,4x plus de chances d'atteindre de meilleures performances de delivery 
→ Google a constaté que 48% des ingénieurs citaient la mauvaise documentation comme leur frein n°1; jusqu'à ce qu'ils placent les docs à côté du code 
→ Le markdown structuré améliore la précision de récupération IA de jusqu'à 35% vs du contenu non structuré

Le pattern qui émerge : documentation as code, directement dans vos repos en markdown. 
Pas seulement le README.md, mais les décisions d'architecture, les guides d'onboarding, les réfèrences API; là où votre assistant IA peut les trouver.

Il ne s'agit pas d'abandonner les wikis. Il s'agit de reconnaître que la proximité du contexte compte plus que jamais quand l'IA est votre pair programmer.

Quelle est l'approche de votre équipe ? Toujours centralisée dans des outils externes, ou docs plus proches du code ? Une approche hybride avec les serveurs MCP ?

🔗 Sources :
DORA Documentation Quality : https://lnkd.in/eAuUcwFT
Google's g3doc journey : https://lnkd.in/enH-MiRb
How to optimize your docs for LLMs :https://lnkd.in/eQySUZjb

#GitHubCopilot #DevEx #DocsAsCode #DeveloperProductivity #AI #SoftwareEngineering #TechnicalWriting #ContextEngineering #GitHub #Microsoft

resharedPost.text:
I Built Context-Aware CI with GitHub Copilot SDK and Microsoft WorkIQ - it's the ability to catch code issues that violate what was said in a meeting, email, etc.

We've all seen it: 2-hour planning meeting, team agrees on PostgreSQL, then someone opens a PR adding MongoDB two weeks later. 40 hours of rework.


The root cause? 

Context fragmentation. Decisions live in Teams transcripts, emails, and "someone's head"—but CI only knows code.

What Microsoft + GitHub makes possible:
An integrated, end-to-end business layer for development context. WorkIQ surfaces your M365 data (meetings, emails, Teams, documents). The GitHub Copilot SDK provides the reasoning & agentic engine. 

Together, they close the loop between organizational decisions and code automatically.

I built a proof-of-concept that changes this:

GitHub Copilot SDK + Microsoft WorkIQ = CI pipeline that validates code PRs against your team's actual decisions.

🔹 Developer wants to merge code via PR
🔹 CI extracts context, queries relevant meeting transcripts
🔹 Copilot SDK analyzes diff against decisions
🔹 Three-state outcome: ✅ PASS / ⚠️ WARN / ❌ FAIL
🔹 PR comment outline the violations to specific meetings as an example.

The result? 

Rework incidents drop. 

Reviews focus on logic, not policy. Institutional knowledge becomes enforceable not just documented.

The future of agentic devOps & CI isn't just testing code — it's validating intent.

 And it's only possible when your business context and the dev toolchain are truly integrated.

My full technical writeup with implementation details is in the thread below 👇

activity_data.text:
Today we’re launching the GitHub Copilot SDK. 

Coding is the first place AI agents have real product-market fit, with daily, sustained use by millions of developers, which means the hardest problems have already been exercised at scale.  

With the Copilot SDK, we’re making that runtime available to every developer, allowing you to embed the same agent execution engine that powers Copilot into any app with just a few lines of code. Planning, execution, tool calling, and controls are designed as a single system, shaped by real workloads and real constraints. We’ve been building toward this across our platforms for some time, and this is the moment those lessons become something the world can build on directly. 

The SDK is available today in technical preview with a GitHub Copilot subscription.

🔗 Learn more about the SDK on the GitHub blog (https://lnkd.in/gHJdS-c5), then visit the SDK repo (https://lnkd.in/gdzVsj-C) to start building.

activity_data.text:
✨ Offsite OCP SaaS : une journée qui nous ressemble ✨

J’ai eu le plaisir d’organiser notre dernier offsite OCP SaaS BNP Paribas, une journée qui me tenait particulièrement à cœur.

Cet événement a été l’occasion d’accueillir 3 nouvelles offres au sein du domaine, ainsi que de nouvelles personnes dans l’équipe, et de les intégrer pleinement autour d’un moment fort de cohésion.

Dès le départ, le mot d’ordre était clair :
👉 cohésion,
👉 partage de notre vision stratégique interne,
👉 et fun.

C’est avec un immense plaisir que nous avons réalisé cet offsite dans les locaux de notre partenaire de longue date Microsoft , que je remercie chaleureusement pour son accueil et son engagement à nos côtés. Mentions spéciales à Loïc Cadiot, Bruno Invernizzi , Oleg M., Tarik Labidi, Tugdual Grall et Thierry Madkaud  pour la gestion de l’organisation et la qualité des présentations. 

La matinée a été rythmée par deux thématiques clés :
🔹 Dynamics 365
🔹 les innovations autour de l’agentique, notamment avec GitHub

Deux présentations et démonstrations animées par les équipes Microsoft, qui ont suscité beaucoup de questions, d’échanges et beaucoup d’intérêt.

L’après-midi, j’ai souhaité aller plus loin avec une approche très concrète : avec l’aide des équipes Microsoft, j’ai imaginé et animé un hackathon — ou plutôt un “agentathon” 🤖
L’objectif : permettre à chaque équipe de développer un jeu vidéo en vibe coding, afin d’illustrer la puissance des agents IA pour construire quelque chose de tangible en seulement quelques heures.

👉 Les résultats ont été bluffants
👉 Des équipes très engagées
👉 Une énergie collective incroyable

Une journée riche en découvertes, en innovation, et surtout axée sur l’humain — des valeurs qui reflètent parfaitement l’ADN de notre domaine OCP SaaS.

Un immense merci à toutes les équipes impliquées.
Hâte de construire la suite ensemble 🚀

Merci Damien Lassalle pour ta confiance sur l’organisation de cette belle journée!  

#BNPPARIBAS #MICROSOFT #Offsite #cohesiondequipe #securitepsychologique #agentique

article.title: Engineering Beyond Agile: AI and the Rise of Agentile teams
article.description: If you’re leading or building in tech, this is the moment to rethink everything you know about engineering organizations. While I don’t have a crystal

activityOfUser.occupation: Senior Strategic Client Advisor – Agentic AI | DevX | Governance | Software

activity_data.text:
AI maturity is forcing a structural rethink of engineering organizations. Agile created speed. Agentile creates intelligence. The shift is already underway.

This moment requires a reconsideration of how engineering organizations operate. In my work as Chief Dev Advisor, the conversations keep converging: #Agentic #DevOps, AI‑assisted coding, and agentic experiences are redefining how teams collaborate, innovate, and deliver value. AI adoption is uneven across the industry, but the direction is uniform. Teams built for this new reality –Agentile teams– combine lean, #agile, and agentic principles to enable humans and AI to work side by side at higher velocity and clarity.

More in the article below. I’m opening this up intentionally. If you’re experimenting with AI in engineering teams – or challenging the old operating models – I welcome your perspectives, examples, and counterpoints.

activity_data.text:
𝗖𝗵𝗮𝘁𝗚𝗣𝗧 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝗽𝗼𝗻𝗱𝘀 𝗶𝗻 𝟮 𝘀𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗱𝘀. 𝗕𝘂𝘁 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗔𝗖𝗧𝗨𝗔𝗟𝗟𝗬 𝗵𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗲𝗻𝘀 𝗯𝗲𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝗰𝗲𝗻𝗲𝘀? 🤖⚡

Most people think it's magic ✨ Engineers know it's a symphony of distributed systems, GPU clusters, and high-dimensional mathematics 🧮

I just published "𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗟𝗶𝗳𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗮𝗻 𝗔𝗜 𝗤𝘂𝗲𝗿𝘆" — a deep dive that traces the millisecond-by-millisecond journey of a single prompt through ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude.

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗵𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗲𝗻𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗮𝘀𝗸: "Write a haiku about a robot loving a cat" 🐱

🔹 Your text is shattered into 𝘁𝗼𝗸𝗲𝗻𝘀 (sub-word units) 
🔹 Each token becomes a 𝘃𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗼𝗿 of thousands of floating-point numbers 
🔹 The Transformer's 𝗠𝘂𝗹𝘁𝗶-𝗛𝗲𝗮𝗱 𝗔𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 calculates relationships 
🔹 The 𝗠𝗟𝗣 Multi-Layer Perceptron retrieves knowledge (robots = metal 🦾, cats = fur 🐈, haikus = 5-7-5) 
🔹 The model predicts 𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝘁𝗼𝗸𝗲𝗻 𝗮𝘁 𝗮 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲 using probability distributions 📊 
🔹 The 𝗞𝗩 𝗖𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗲 prevents redundant calculations 🚀

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗥𝗲𝘀𝘂𝗹𝘁: "Metal heart beats fast / Soft fur purrs against the steel / Love knows no code base" 💙

𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗹𝗼𝗼𝗸𝘀 𝗹𝗶𝗸𝗲 𝗶𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝘁 𝗺𝗮𝗴𝗶𝗰 𝗶𝘀 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆:
⚙️ JSON payloads hitting edge nodes 
⚙️ TLS-encrypted routing to GPU clusters
⚙️ Gigabytes of model weights in HBM 
⚙️ 96 stacked Transformer layers 
⚙️ Positional encodings preserving word order 
⚙️ Autoregressive loops generating text token-by-token

This isn't just a curiosity question. 𝗜𝘁'𝘀 𝗮 𝘀𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺 𝗱𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻 𝗾𝘂𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻. 💡

If you're building with AI, debugging model behavior, or just want to understand what's under the hood — this one's for you 👇

📖 Read the full breakdown (with diagrams): https://lnkd.in/gYDEKg-4

Like this? Reshare and follow me (Priyanka) for more such cloud and AI tips!

#AI #MachineLearning #SystemDesign #LLM #ChatGPT #Claude #Gemini #TechDeepDive #DeepLearning #Transformers

One of my personal highlights was having the chance to host and moderate our customer insight panel. I’m incredibly grateful to:
• Jesada Gonkratoke – SCB TechX

A huge thank you to our #GitHub and #Microsoft teams and to our partners Fusion Solution Co., Ltd. and PALO IT for the strong collaboration. And thank you to every customer who joined us — your engagement and feedback are what make events like this so meaningful.

activity_data.text:
AI is causing a new dev pattern: I heard this yesterday: I show my stakeholders a demo, we generate 10-12 exciting ideas, and by end of next day, 80% are already in production. Then I let them know: 'It's live — what do you think?"

People who fret about AI replacing dev jobs are missing the point. When you can create value 10x or even 100x more quickly, you get more headcount, not less.

Another example: I paired with someone two days ago who made a circa-2005 Google Analytics clone in less than 30 minutes. (One JavaScript snippet, sending events to Google Cloud Run, PubSub, and BigQuery.). It will eventually ingest 20MM events/month, way above the 1MM Google Analytics unsampled limit.  (We wrote the reporting engine in Python Streamlit today.)

The longest part of the process? Getting permission to create PubSub subscribers. Not coding.

These types of obstacles are what Steve Yegge and I called "barbed wire" in our Vibe Coding book. But I never saw as vividly as how critical it is to have someone from infosec or admin rights on the team — all your wildest dreams are blocked by waiting to get access rights.

Another heartbreaking form of barbed wire? People stuck in protracted sprint planning processes, endless backlog grooming meetings about what features should work on next and which need to be pushed into next quarter... 

The new bottlenecks are organizational, not technical — let's go fix them!

activity_data.text:
What if your AI assistant could follow your rules, reflect your stack, and automate your engineering workflows with precision?

💡GitHub just introduced custom agents in Copilot, enabling teams to extend AI into observability, IaC, security, databases, deployments, incident response and more. These agents are defined in plain Markdown and available across Copilot CLI, VS Code, and github.com. 

Each custom agent is built from a clear set of components that shape how it behaves inside your environment:
• A detailed definition that outlines the agent’s purpose, capabilities, and behavior
• A set of MCP tools the agent can work with, providing structured and secure access to systems and APIs
• Metainformation for handoffs, allowing agents to guide Copilot into the next logical workflow step and support multi-step tasks

This structure moves AI-native development from concept to practice. Teams can encode knowledge, maintain standards, and orchestrate workflows through reusable agent specifications tailored to their engineering context.

Learn more and start using custom agents today: https://msft.it/6047tcHKV

#GitHub #GitHubCopilot #AINative #MCP #CustomAgents

article.title: Software Engineer (Quality), Retail and Marcom Engineering - Jobs - Careers at Apple

activityOfUser.occupation: Engineering | Systems and Software Design | Networking Software | Microsoft | Co-founder and Advisor @Vahati for Women in STEM Empowerment

activity_data.text:
I don't post much here — mostly when I'm hiring. So if you're seeing this, hi. 👋

I'm looking for a Senior Software Engineer to join my team at Apple, and I want to give you an honest picture of what the role actually is — because the title will mislead you.

It says "Quality." It is not a QA job.

What it actually is: you'd be building the platform infrastructure that Retail & Marcom Engineering relies on to ship confidently at scale — across 100+ languages, millions of customers, and some of Apple's highest-visibility launches.

That means shared CI/CD services, LLM-powered developer tooling (RAG copilots, agentic test generation, natural-language CI gates), federated APIs, and the evaluation harnesses that make it all trustworthy. Real platform engineering. Real AI work, not just the word.

The kind of engineer I'm hoping to find has shipped something LLM-powered in a production context, has opinions about CI flakiness and runner infrastructure, and wants to shape platform direction — not just close tickets.

Node or Python (ideally both), 5+ years of experience, and at least one shipped AI feature are the core bar. Seniority preference is 8+ years with platform or automation depth.

If this sounds like you, apply here: https://lnkd.in/gS68g-YP

And if you know someone who'd be a great fit, I'd appreciate a share.

article.title: Solutions Architect - 2026 (US)

activityOfUser.occupation: Engineering | Systems and Software Design | Networking Software | Microsoft | Co-founder and Advisor @Vahati for Women in STEM Empowerment

activity_data.text:
🚀 Exciting Opportunity for Recent Grads: Associate Solutions Architect at AWS 🎓 

Are you passionate about AI and cloud technology? Ready to launch your career at the forefront of innovation? Amazon Web Services is seeking talented early-career technologists for an Associate Solutions Architect position starting May 11, 2026.

Locations: Arlington, VA | Austin, TX | Seattle, WA

Why This Role?
This is your opportunity to work alongside experienced AWS architects and learn how to design real-world cloud and AI architectures used by some of the most innovative companies in the world. You'll work directly with customers ranging from startups to global enterprises, helping them architect scalable, resilient solutions that solve real business problems. You'll be a trusted customer advocate, guiding organizations through cloud and AI adoption and best practices.

AI at the Core
We're especially excited about candidates with experience in AI, Large Language Models (LLMs), and Machine Learning. As AI continues to reshape every industry, you'll be at the center of helping organizations harness these transformative technologies on AWS.

What You'll Do:
✅  Design, architect, and prototype solutions that address complex customer challenges
✅  Evangelize AWS services and best practices across diverse organizations
✅  Partner with developers, architects, and business stakeholders to accelerate cloud and AI adoption
✅  Translate customer feedback into actionable requirements for product teams

What We're Looking For:
• Bachelor's degree in Computer Science, Computer Engineering, or related field (graduation date May 2024 - April 2026)
• Programming experience (Java, Python, Ruby, Node.js, Go, Rust, C#)
• Experience building applications with AI, including LLMs or generative AI models
• Excellent communication skills for technical and non-technical audiences, including public speaking
• Background in analytics, security, storage, DevOps, or application development

Bonus Points:
• AWS or industry certifications
• Experience with frameworks, design patterns, and software delivery

Start Date: May 11, 2026

This is your chance to build the future developing both your technical expertise and business acumen. If you're a self-starter ready to make a significant impact and help customers craft highly scalable, flexible cloud architectures, we want to hear from you!

#AWS #CloudComputing #SolutionsArchitect #AI #GenerativeAI #MachineLearning #CareerOpportunity #TechJobs #CloudArchitecture #Innovation #ArlingtonVA #AustinTX #SeattleWA

Interested? Apply now and come build the future with us! 🌟
