activity_data.text:
How to use AI for your next job interview

Noam Segal, my community research lead, interviewed 30 current and recent job seekers about how they use AI throughout the interview process.

What he found went far beyond polishing resumes. People had built entire systems tailor-made for their own situations: ways to get feedback on what they actually said in interviews, methods to predict questions before walking in, workflows to surface stories they didn’t know they had. Each person he spoke with had figured out how to use AI for one or two pieces of the interviewing puzzle.

As he was pulling together a research report from these conversations, he quickly realized that most people on the job market are stressed and anxious enough. The best value he could offer wasn’t a list of tips but, instead, a way to plug-and-play the hard work these participants have already done. 

So he changed direction, took every interview AI technique that worked for these participants, added a layer of professional coaching techniques, and built a free Claude Code–based coach you can plug-and-play into your interview process today.

The coach helps you with every step of the process:
1. Scores your actual interview responses (not what you think you said)
2. Mines your experience for stories you didn't know you had
3. Tracks the gap between how you think you did and how you actually did
4. Runs mock interviews that push back and interrupt you
5. Coaches post-offer salary negotiation with exact scripts and fallback language
...and much more

Grab it here (totally free): https://lnkd.in/gGnQa5iE

To the engineers, product managers, and customer-focused builders in logistics tech: we're hiring.

There's a narrative taking hold in the tech sector right now that the era of the human builder is shrinking. 

That as AI advances, the need for brilliant engineers, product managers, and support teams simply goes away.

At Clear.ai, we take a fundamentally different view.

Building true AI-first software doesn't reduce the need for great engineering and deep product empathy — it elevates it. It enables humans to focus on what only they can do best: solving complex, high-leverage problems, thinking creatively, and shipping incredible user experiences.

There's been a lot of movement in the logistics tech market this week. But we believe the future of logistics needs more builders, not fewer. 

If that's the kind of team you want to be part of, let's talk.

activity_data.text:
🚀 We’re Hiring: Product Manager Analyst – ML Digital Experience & Digital Workplace

Are you passionate about building intelligent, data-driven products that transform how teams work? Do you love solving complex problems at the intersection of product, data, and machine learning? If so, we want to meet you!

Our ML Digital Experience & Digital Workplace team is growing, and we’re looking for a Product Manager Analyst who will help drive the strategy, development, and optimization of ML-powered workplace solutions used across the organization.

In this role, you’ll act as the bridge between business stakeholders, engineers, and data scientists—ensuring we deliver products that boost productivity, empower employees, and elevate the digital workplace.

🔍 What You’ll Do
✨ Drive product strategy and help shape roadmaps for innovative ML-driven solutions
📊 Analyze data, build dashboards, and uncover insights that guide key product decisions
🤝 Partner closely with ML engineers + data science teams to define models and enhance performance
📣 Collaborate with cross-functional stakeholders and present product updates to leadership
🧩 Advocate for exceptional user experiences through research, testing, and feedback loops
⚙️ Identify opportunities for automation and digital workplace optimization

🎓 What We’re Looking For
✔️ 2–5 years in product management, product/business analysis, or similar roles
✔️ Strong hands-on experience with analytics tools (Power BI, Tableau, Databricks, SQL)
✔️ Foundational understanding of ML/AI concepts
✔️ Excellent communication skills and the ability to work in an Agile environment
✔️ A passion for turning complex data into actionable decisions

⭐ Bonus Points For
Experience with digital workplace tools (Microsoft 365, ServiceNow, VDI environments, collaboration platforms)
Familiarity with telemetry/enterprise usage analytics
Exposure to NLP, recommendation engines, or predictive models
Product management certifications
Experience in healthcare or large enterprise environments

If you're excited about shaping the future of the digital workplace and want to work in a fast-paced, collaborative environment that values innovation—we’d love to connect with you!

https://lnkd.in/d5QTwVH6

👉 Apply today and help us build smarter, more intuitive workplace experiences powered by machine learning.

Trust CX Innovations at Amazon is looking for a Senior Technical Product Manager to help define what it means for AI to truly earn people's trust.

As part of our US team, you'll lead product strategy at the intersection of privacy, accessibility, and responsible AI — shaping how hundreds of millions of people experience Alexa+ and Amazon's connected devices. ✅ Scheduler scalability and efficiency (hybrid client → multi-socket servers)
✅ OS security features
✅ Foundational kernel components (sync primitives, timers, registry)

🚀 We're hiring! Join us at the cutting edge of intelligent UX — where AI meets modern web development.

The Fluent Hub team in the Office Product Group is expanding! We're looking for a Software Engineer to help shape the future of Fluent UI React v9, the component library behind Microsoft 365 experiences like Office, Teams, Outlook, and Copilot.

But that’s just the beginning.

We're building out a GenUI platform that leverages LLMs to make Fluent UI smarter and more adaptive. At the heart of this is our evolving design system knowledge base — a unified source of truth that helps engineers, designers, and PMs generate code, explore Fluent capabilities, and create intelligent user interfaces with greater confidence and speed.

🔍 What you’ll work on:
- Evolving our LLM-powered Fluent knowledge base into a cross domain unified source of truth.
- Designing model evaluation pipelines and exploring generative UX opportunities.
- Contributing to React components used across M365.
- Collaborating across disciplines in a culture that values inclusion, growth, and innovation.

📍 Based in Prague, in the Czech Republic with flexible work-from-home options.

If you're passionate about AI, React, and redefining how we build user interfaces — we’d love to hear from you.

Apply here 👉 : https://lnkd.in/eBgVezyD 

#Microsoft #Prague #FluentUI #React #GenUI #AI #UX #SoftwareEngineering #Hiring #LLM

resharedPost.text:
This weekend we ran our first Garaža Frontier Hackathon!

21 teams / 80 students were building AI products for 40 hours in the Tenderly Belgrade office. Teams worked end to end from problem framing and technical execution to demos and pitches. The level of ideas and execution was consistently strong across teams.

The three prizes went to teams: 
Deepgrad – 1st place
Cascada – 2nd place
Srećan Kraj – 3rd place

Prizes were provided by Tenderly, Perplexity, Kortix, and Solflare, and we're thankful for their continued support of early stage builders. Teams were also supported by Insightful and LAUNCHub Ventures.

Perplexity, ElevenLabs, Lovable, Daytona and fal provided API credits and bounty awards.

We were happy to have mentors from the Garaža community over the weekend, spending time with teams and giving valuable feedback: George Dimitrijevic, Ivan Ralic, Luka Filipovic, Boris Janković and Miloš Zlatković.

We had 12 VCs visiting Belgrade for the Demo Day: LAUNCHub Ventures, Credo Ventures, Inovo.vc, Day One Capital, South Central Ventures, KAYA VC, Omorika Ventures, PortfoLion Capital Partners, Market One Capital, SQ Capital, Silicon Gardens, Constructor Capital.

A big thank you to the volunteers who supported the teams and helped keep everything running smoothly throughout the 2 days.

Special thanks to the Tenderly team for hosting us and helping organize the hackathon from start to finish!

Photo: Nemanja Rajić

activityOfUser.occupation: Software Engineer @ Microsoft

activity_data.text:
I have been part of more than 1000+ interviews (500+ at amazon and ~500 in different settings) 
I'm going to share some of the most important things which I learned from these interviews. 

🧩 Crack the System Design Interview — Part 1 of 20

What Interviewers Are Actually Scoring You On 
Most engineers walk into a system design interview believing one thing:
"If I get the architecture right, I pass."
I used to think the same thing - that belief alone has cost some very talented engineers the job.
Here's the truth nobody tells you upfront.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
The interview isn't about the perfect design.
There is no perfect design. Every system has trade-offs. Every architecture decision you make solves one problem and creates another. Interviewers know this. What they're really watching is how you think, not what you know.
I've seen engineers sketch a flawless distributed system and still fail. I've also seen engineers with a messy whiteboard get the offer. The difference wasn't talent. It was how they communicated.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
 Here's what's actually being evaluated:
- Problem Clarity Do you ask the right questions before jumping in? Or do you assume you already know what needs to be built?
- Structured Thinking Is your approach organized and easy to follow? Can the interviewer see where you're going?
- Trade-off Awareness Do you acknowledge what you're giving up with each decision? Every choice has a cost. Senior engineers name it.
- Communication Can you explain your thinking clearly while you're still figuring things out? This one is underrated and it matters enormously.
- Adaptability When the interviewer pushes back or changes a constraint, do you adapt — or do you freeze?
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
The pattern I see most often:
An engineer spends 40 minutes building a beautiful system. Never once explains why they made a choice. Never asks a clarifying question. And when the interviewer says "what if we needed 10x the traffic?" — they fall apart.

That's not a knowledge problem. That's a communication problem.

The best candidates I've seen treat the interview like a live problem-solving session with a colleague. They think out loud. They say things like "I'm going with a relational database here because consistency matters more than scale for this use case — but I'd revisit that if we hit 50 million users." That one sentence tells me more than a perfect diagram ever could.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

The three questions every interviewer is quietly asking themselves:
❓ Would I want to work through a hard problem with this person? 
❓ Do they stay clear and calm under pressure? 
❓ Do they know what they don't know — and are they honest about it?

If you can make an interviewer answer yes to all three, the technical details almost take care of themselves.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
#SystemDesign

1. Cut your alcohol intake

2. Don't drink your calories.

20. Track your workouts to stay motivated.

25. Getting super shredded boosts your confidence like completing an ultra marathon.

28. Discipline = Freedom

29. There will be bumps in the road, progress is never linear.

30. Have protein with every meal.

31. Eat your protein first and you're less likely to overeat.

32. Go for a walk outdoors, every day.

35. High-quality protein powder and eggs are less work than chewing meat.

36. Meat keeps you fuller longer.

40. Eat like shit = Feel like shit

47. Filling your home with nutrient-dense, protein-rich, and belly-filling foods is ultimate self-care.

activity_data.text:
3 ways to get into product management without doing an MBA!

I often get asked if pursuing an MBA is necessary to break into product management and the short answer is NO!

An MBA can help by giving you a deep understanding of business concepts and strategies, a strong network, and job placement opportunities. But it could take 2 years and cost more than $200K to get an MBA from a top tier program.

The good news is that there are several ways to break into product management, and MBA is just one of them. Here are a few more options:

✅ Transitioning into a PM role in your current company: Great option if you’re already working in a tech company, have a stellar performance history, and have some experience with product development. 

Tips:
🎯 Enquire about formal PM rotations in your company
🎯 Raise your hand for stretch PM assignments
🎯 Network with your current colleagues and managers, and discuss your product management aspirations and readiness.

✅ APM programs: APM (Associate Product Manager) programs are highly competitive 2–3 year programs where the company provides you with on the job training and mentoring you need to become a successful product manager. 

Tips:
🎯 Research the different programs, and tailor your applications to each program. 
🎯 Pay attention to application deadlines
🎯 Doing side projects and highlighting transferable skills on your resume would strengthen your candidacy for such programs.

✅ Applying for regular PM jobs: You don't have to go back to school in this option, but it is still a lot of work and it could be difficult to get your foot in the door without any experience. 

Tips
🎯 Proactively network with people in the field and be ready to talk about your skills and experience
🎯 Build your portfolio of side projects that demonstrate PM skills
🎯 Take on volunteer work that can help develop and showcase PM skills and abilities.

Whichever route you choose, the key is to stay focused on your goals, develop relevant skills, and build relationships in the field.

Add your tips in comments, ask me more questions and don't forget to share this with someone who can benefit from this post!

#productmanagement #mbajobs #apm #entryway #careerguide

activity_data.text:
If you're going to mock interview

Here's what you need to know

First stop mock interviewing 

• To memorize answers
• To learn data structures
• To get a company referral

Memorizing the answer to 1000+ possible questions won't help you.

You have a lot of time to learn data structures on your own.

Not everyone is comfortable giving a referral to their workplace.

Instead, use mock interviews to practice

• Thinking out loud
• Pointing out edge cases
• Outlining your algorithm
• Communicating effectively
• Asking clarifying questions
• Proactively testing your code
• Handling interview stress and anxiety
• Discussing multiple solution tradeoffs

Whether I was at Microsoft, Amazon, or Google. These topics were always part of the interviewing rubric.

Most interviewees do well on coding.

You can take it a step further doing well on communicating.

Good luck.

#softwareengineer
#coding
#alexcancode
